role_summary: SNCC program coordinator and Howard University NAG member; coordinated transport for the 1963 March on Washington; Lowndes County Freedom Party organizer with Stokely Carmichael.
biographical_paragraph: At Howard University in the early 1960s, Courtland Cox joined the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG), the campus arm of SNCC, and in 1962 helped organize Project Awareness, the speaker series that brought Malcolm X to campus for a debate [src: 1]. Cox was born January 27, 1941; he had come to Howard in 1960 after St. Helena's Catholic School [src: 1]. He served as NAG's representative on SNCC's coordinating council, and later as a SNCC program coordinator; in that role he ran the transportation logistics for the August 1963 March on Washington [src: 1]. In 1964 he was part of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's challenge at the Atlantic City Democratic National Convention, an experience that in his own account convinced him of the need for an independent Black political party [src: 1]. He went next to Lowndes County, Alabama, joining Stokely Carmichael in organizing the Lowndes County Freedom Party; in the months after the Voting Rights Act passed, the project registered some 2,800 voters and shifted the county's rolls from majority-white to majority-Black [src: 1]. In 1967 Cox represented SNCC at the Bertrand Russell International War Crimes Tribunal in Stockholm, opposing the Vietnam War; in 1974 he helped organize the Sixth Pan-African Congress in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania [src: 1]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 24 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 2].

sources.title: Courtland Cox oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
